content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 12, 2017

Michael Torres
Assistant City Attorney
City of Newport Beach
100 Civic Center Drive
Newport Beach, CA 92660

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-117

Dear Mr. Torres:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Planning Commission Chairman Kory Kramer regarding his duties under the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

May Chairman Kramer participate in governmental decisions involving the revitalization of Mariners' Mile in Newport Beach despite the fact that his employer has a business relationship with a hotel in the revitalization area and the fact that he sits on the Board of Directors of a second business that owns the hotel?

CONCLUSION

No. Based on the Chairman's interests (as discussed below) the Chairman has a conflict of interest in the decisions.

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-117
Page No. 2

FACTS

Chairman Kramer serves as the Chairman of the Newport Beach Planning Commission. He
has served as a planning commissioner since July of 2011 and his current term ends June 30, 2019.

Interest in Pacific Hospitality Group Ventures, Inc. ("PHG")

• In his private capacity, Chairman Kramer is the Chief Investment Officer ("CIO") of a
privately-owned California corporation, Pacific Hospitality Group Ventures, Inc.
("PHG"). PHG has annual revenue of more than $5 million.

• As the CIO of PHG, he receives a salary in an amount that has exceeded $500 in the past
12 months.

• Chairman Kramer does not have any ownership interest in PHG, which is wholly owned
by a single shareholder.

• PHG is involved in the operation, development, ownership, and management of a
collection of hotel properties throughout California, including the Balboa Bay Resort,
which is located within Marinrs' Mile in Newport Beach.

Interest in International Bay Clubs ("IBC")

• Chairman Kramer serves on the Board of Directors of IBC, which owns and manages
the Balboa Bay Resort. Chairman Kramer does not have any ownership interest in, nor
does he receive a salary from, the IBC or the Balboa Bay Resort.

• On May 23, 2017, you stated that IBC and PHG have no financial or ownership
relationship with one another.

• On May 31, 2017, you clarified that there are nine members on the IBC board (including
Mr. Kramer), and a total of three of the nine members of the board are PHG employees
(including Mr. Kramer).

• Neither PHG, IBC, nor the Balboa Bay Resort has initiated the Marinrs' Mile Project
nor will they be named parties in, or the subject of, the project decisions.

The City is in the beginning stages of developing a revitalization plan for Mariners' Mile,
which is a one and half mile stretch of Pacific Coast Highway between Dover Drive and North
Newport Boulevard in the City of Newport Beach. The Balboa Bay Resort is located within
Mariners' Mile at 1221 West Coast Highway, Newport Beach, California. The revitalization plan,
among other things, will explore opportunities to expand Pacific Coast Highway, implement new
parking standards, and provide new pedestrian and streetscape improvements and beautification
projects.

File No. A-17-117
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 provides:

"No public official at any level of state or local government shall make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to
influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know
he has a financial interest."

The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to conflict of interests arising from
certain enumerated interests described in Section 87103. A public official has an interest in the
following:

• A business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more.
(Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)) or in which he or she is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section
87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)

• Any source of income, including promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months
prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• Any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts total $470 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(e).)

• His or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her
immediate family. This is commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87103.)

Chairman Kramer has an interest in his employer PHG as a business entity (Section
87103(d)) and as a source of income (Section 87103(c)). Chairman Kramer also has an interest in
IBC as a business entity pursuant to Section 87103(d) based on his position as a member of the
Board of Directors.

Foreseeability

A public official has a financial interest in a government decision, within the meaning of the
Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the
official, the official's immediate family, or the official's interests, and it is distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.

Under the Act, an effect on an interest is presumed foreseeable if the interest is explicitly
involved in the decision, and an interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in,
or subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18701(a).) You stated, neither of the Chairman's financial
interests, PHG nor IBC, has initiated the Mariners' Mile Project nor will they be named parties in,

File No. A-17-117
Page No. 4

or the subject of, the project decisions. Therefore, an effect of the decision on the Chairman's
interests is only reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Materiality

1. Special Nexus Rule: Regulation 18702.3(c) provides:

"Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person who is a source
of income to a public official is deemed material if the public official receives or
is promised the income to achieve a goal or purpose which would be achieved,
defeated, aided, or hindered by the decision."

The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to accomplish a purpose
that may be advanced by what he or she does as a public official, we presume that the private
employer is benefiting from the actions of the employee in his or her official capacity. (Yarnell
Advice Letter, No. A-00-161.)

Typically, a "nexus" is found in situations where the official is also a high-level employee
with direct influence and control over his or her employer's management or policy decisions.²

Here, the Chairman is CIO at PHG (PHG is involved in operation, development, ownership,
and management of the Balboa Bay Resort), and a Board Member of IBC (IBC owns the Balboa
Bay Resort). The Balboa Bay Resort is located in (and does business from) Mariners' Mile, the area
subject to the proposed revitalization plan in question.

2. Materiality Standard: Financial Interests in Business Entities: In addition, the Act and its
interpretive regulations provide different general materiality standards that apply to determine
whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an interest will be material. (Regulation
18702.) Regulation 18702.1 applies to interests in business entities.³ Generally, a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on a business entity in which the official has an interest (and that is not
explicitly involved in a decision), is material:

"[I]f a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a
change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of
a privately held business entity." (Regulations 18702.1(b) and 18702.3(a)(4).)

² Examples include, Moser Advice Letter, No. A-03-147 (nexus applied to a chief operating officer); Tran
Advice Letter, No. A-16-024 and Todorov Advice Letter, A-16-020 (nexus applied to Chief Executive Officers);
Khalife Advice Letter, No. A-15-243 (nexus applied to an Executive Director), Brown Advice Letter, No. I-15-179
(nexus applied to a Co-President); Ayres Advice Letter, No. A-14-176 (nexus applied to nonprofit campaign director);
Furek Advice Letter, No. A-14-074 (nexus applied to President and CEO), and the Low Advice Letter, No. A-99-304,
(nexus applied to a Programs Director).

³ Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the standard for materiality applicable to the effect of a decision on an official's
interest in a source of income (such as PHIG), however it provides that if the source of income is a business entity,
materiality is also determined pursuant to the standards of Regulation 18702.1.

File No. A-17-117
Page No. 5

The revitalization plan in question, among other things, will explore opportunities to expand
Pacific Coast Highway, implement new parking standards, and provide new pedestrian and
streetscape improvements and beautification projects. A prudent person would find it reasonably
foreseeable that significant revitalization of the Mariner Mile area around the Balboa Beach Resort
would have a substantial effect on the value of PHG and IBC. Moreover, since the Chairman holds
a high level position at both PHG and IBC, he would have a nexus in the revitalization decisions.
Therefore, the Chairman has a conflict of interest in the revitalization decisions and may not
participate.⁴

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division

JWW:jgl

_______________________________________________________________________________

⁴ Note that when a public official holds an office specified in Section 87200 (such as a planning commissioner)
and has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, he or she must: (1) immediately prior to the
discussion of the item, orally identify each type of interest involved in the decision as well as details of the interest on
the record of the meeting, (2) recuse himself or herself, and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and or or
vote on the item. (Section 87105: Regulation 18707.)
